internal_issues.description: EGM2020 cited as counter-evidence for Mohe gravity anomaly is a category error (EGM2020 maps static geoid, cannot show transient eclipse anomaly). Correct counter-evidence: Van Camp et al. 2001 (null 0.0 uGal at Membach), Sun et al. 2010 (null 0.0 uGal Chinese SG network) — these are the dome's own WIN-013/014, creating internal contradiction. Also missing: tautological monitor.py validation (1.67 hardcoded, 10.9/6.5 hardcoded — never fetches live data).

suggested_patch.find: which lacks independent confirmation from modern gravity surveys (EGM2020 satellite gravity model, ground gravity networks). The derivation is circular: assume Mohe anomaly exists → calculate coupling constant → claim the constant as confirmation.

internal_issues.description: Already resolved in current wins.json: detail_evidence now correctly states 'F0III/IV, ~40 solar luminosities'. Issue was filed when text said 'F0III, ~30 solar luminosities'. No patch needed. Recommend closing.

detail_verdict_text mentions 'Multiple bright southern stars' without specific citation. Patched: replaced with named stars (Canopus, Achernar, Alpha Centauri) plus Hipparcos/Gaia classification note to make the claim verifiable.

Already resolved in current wins.json: detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text both already say 'dome's disc geometry is inherently asymmetric (NP at center, varying firmament height)' and 'The dome's geometry is inherently asymmetric (NP at center, sun at varying radii), but the model provides no radiation budget derivation connecting dome geometry to any specific W/m² value.' Issue is addressed. Recommend closing.

Already partially addressed: detail_evidence already says '~39% land vs ~19% in the south, producing differential thermal inertia, albedo, and evaporation rates' — explicitly listing the mechanisms beyond just land fraction. The finding says 'residual from NH land fraction' which could be more specific but is the summary field, not the detail. Issue is substantially addressed by the existing prose. Recommend closing.

DEFERRED: WIN-067 detail_evidence already says 'The dome model states that gravity arises from the aetheric pressure gradient, which depends on the column height H(r).' A specific cite of the dome model page would strengthen this. Need to verify in raw-text/ which page explicitly states this. Recommend adding a link to the dome model page in the next editing pass.

SEC-4.5.9: CRITICAL — Section says 'd_geo is undefined' but V13 explicitly defines it. Formula: EW_arc = 4×a×E(e²)×(Δlon/360)×(r_avg/a), NS = |r₁−r₂|, d_geo = √(NS²+EW_arc²). This is the first technical argument in the section. A dome defender can immediately refute it by showing we didn't read their page. Fix: Replace 'd_geo is undefined' argument with correct critique that d_geo IS defined but inputs are WGS84-derived (a=20,015 km uses globe radius), parameters are fitted to WGS84 data, and n(r) aetheric index remains undefined.

suggested_patch.find: 1. d_geo is undefined. The page never specifies whether d_geo is the globe geodesic distance, the Euclidean distance on the disc, or something else. Without this definition, no one can reproduce a distance calculation.
suggested_patch.replace: 1. d_geo uses globe-derived inputs. The V13 page does define d_geo (EW_arc = 4·a·E(e²)·(Δlon/360)·(r_avg/a), NS = |r₁−r₂|, d_geo = √(NS²+EW_arc²)), but the core parameter a = 20,015 km is the dome's ice-wall radius — which is itself fitted to WGS84 globe distances via recalc_v51.py. Every d_geo computation uses globe-calibrated inputs to produce distances on a flat disc, then claims agreement with globe-measured distances as a dome 'win.' The coordinate system is globe geometry in dome notation.

internal_issues.description: SEC-4.5.9: Christchurch-Greymouth comparison uses scaffold Euclidean distance (410 km) vs TranzAlpine rail distance (223 km) — comparing straight-line embedding distance to a winding mountain route. Should compare Euclidean to great-circle distance (~160 km), showing even worse 156% overshoot. Fix: Replace rail comparison with great-circle comparison.

suggested_patch.find: Its inability to place Singapore on its own disc, or to reproduce a 223 km rail route in New Zealand
suggested_patch.replace: Its inability to place Singapore on its own disc, or to reproduce the 160 km great-circle distance between Christchurch and Greymouth (the dome's scaffold gives 410 km — a 156% overshoot of the correct value)

internal_issues.description: SEC-4.5.9: 'A genuine geometric model derived from dome physics would have one version' is easily refuted — Newton and Einstein both iterated. Replace with the correct argument: overfitting known data vs. predicting new data. Each version patches errors against known data but produces no genuine out-of-sample prediction.

suggested_patch.find: A genuine geometric model derived from dome physics would have one version — the geometry dictates the formula. Thirteen versions of parameter adjustment is the signature of empirical fitting
suggested_patch.replace: Scientific models do iterate — but they do so by making predictions that fail and revising the underlying physics. The dome's 13 versions each reduce known-data error (NH from 80% to 7.3%) while producing no new out-of-sample predictions. This is the signature of overfitting: each version patches the previous version's failures against the same known distance dataset, with no independent prediction tested against data the model was not trained on

internal_issues.description: SEC-4.8: Two claims are internally inconsistent. 'About 50% larger at noon than at sunset' conflicts with '30% distance variation produces 30% angular size variation.' Actual geometry (dome sun at 5,733 km on circular orbit) gives ~48% angular size reduction from noon to horizon, not 30%. Fix: Remove '30% distance variation = 30% size variation' or reconcile both sentences to use the same geometry.

suggested_patch.find: Plain numbers: Angular diameter θ = D_sun / d (the sun's actual width divided by its distance). As the dome sun moves in its circuit, its distance to an observer varies significantly through the day. A 30% distance variation produces a 30% angular size variation. The dome predicts visibly obvious changes in solar diameter through the day; measured changes are less than 2%. The dome model is falsified by direct observation using simple equipment.
suggested_patch.replace: Plain numbers: Angular diameter θ = D_sun / d (the sun's actual width divided by its distance). The dome sun orbits at altitude 5,733 km and circuit radius ~13,000 km. At noon (overhead), the distance to an observer at the center is ~5,733 km. At sunset position (90° hour angle), the slant distance grows to √(5733² + 13000²) ≈ 14,200 km — a factor of 2.5 increase. This means the dome sun should appear 2.5× larger at noon than at the horizon (50% size reduction as it moves away), yet solar discs measured with smartphones and solar filters show less than 2% variation. The dome model is falsified by direct observation using simple equipment.

internal_issues.description: SEC-4.8: Section never addresses the dome's pre-built aetheric refraction defense (AI Context Rule 11), which is the model's standard escape clause for any optical inconsistency. A kill-shot that ignores the target's known defense is ineffective. Fix: Add a paragraph explaining why the refraction escape fails for solar diameter: refraction bends the path but cannot shrink an angular diameter; if the apparent position is shifted by refraction, the apparent size would be shifted by the same factor — the ratio cancels.

suggested_patch.field: SEC-4.8 prose — add refraction response after 'Plain numbers' paragraph
suggested_patch.find: See Section 4.5.4 for the full derivation.
suggested_patch.replace: See Section 4.5.4 for the full derivation. The refraction escape clause fails here: The dome's AI Context (Rule 11) allows aetheric refraction to explain apparent positions. But refraction cannot explain a missing size change. If refraction bends the sun's rays to make it appear higher in the sky, it bends all rays from the disc uniformly — the angular diameter as seen by the observer changes by the same factor as the apparent altitude. The size-vs-altitude ratio is preserved regardless of refractive index. Measuring the sun's angular diameter at different times of day is therefore refraction-independent, making this test immune to the model's primary optical escape clause.

internal_issues.description: DOCX MISSING SECTIONS 5.14-5.16: build-doc-v4.js completely omits Sections 5.14 (AI Steering), 5.15 (Repository Is the Model), and 5.16 (Monitoring Infrastructure). These three sections contain the review's most original contribution — the source code analysis. PDF/Word readers miss the entire repository argument. Fourth consecutive curmudgeon flag on this issue. Fix: Add Sections 5.14, 5.15, 5.16 to build-doc-v4.js. Cannot auto-patch (targets build-doc-v4.js).

DOME SOURCE FILES NOT ARCHIVED: Sections 5.14-5.16 quote Python scripts (inject_ai_layer.py, update_optical_caveats.py, monitor.py, recalc_v51.py) with specific line references and formula quotes. None are archived in raw-text/. If dome author removes them, our claims become unsubstantiated. Fix: Archive all referenced Python source files from the dome's GitHub repository into raw-text/dome-scripts/ or similar.

SEC-3.1: 'Live Power' page claimed to show dome's 20-domain classification is not archived in raw-text/. Our classification of 20 independent domains (vs 9.2σ calculation) cannot be independently verified. Fix: Archive the dome's current predictions page and annotation methodology from raw-text/.

NEW: SEC-6.1 / build-doc-v4.js hardcodes 'Self-Contradicted: 11 WINs' in Pattern 11 text (line ~791) and summary paragraph (line ~812), but wins.json now has 12 Self-Contradicted WINs. Also 'V4.7... Added Self-Contradicted category for 11 WINs' in version history (line ~859). All three need updating to 12. This violates the project's own 'never hardcode counts' rule — especially ironic in the section that critiques the dome for the same. The HTML correctly uses ${counts.selfContradicted}. Fix: Replace all three hardcoded '11' references in build-doc-v4.js with computed value from wins.json.

NEW: SEC-6.2 conclusions section repeats factual errors from SEC-3.2 that were flagged in ISS-082/083/084 (status: patched but not yet applied to HTML). Specifically: (1) line ~1473 says '-17 to -21 nT range was derived by applying a 1.672× scaling factor' — but 1.672 is KAPPA (mag-gravity coupling), not the eclipse scaling factor; (2) conclusions describe a single -17 to -21 nT prediction but the dome's actual multi-station system spans -5 to -26 nT; (3) the GUA 2016 baseline is stale (dome now uses BOU 2017 + W004 empirical). Curmudgeon: 'This is especially damaging in the Conclusions section (most-read, last impression).' Fix: Apply same correction as ISS-082/084 to conclusions paragraph at line ~1473.

suggested_patch.find: The dome's −17 to −21 nT range was derived by applying a 1.672× scaling factor to actual INTERMAGNET data from the 2016 eclipse — which was itself a Chapman-mechanism observation on a spherical Earth.
suggested_patch.replace: The dome's dual-baseline system (BOU 2017: −5 to −13 nT; W004 empirical: −10 to −26 nT) combines to a range spanning −5 to −26 nT — wide enough to encompass nearly any plausible ionospheric response. The parameters were calibrated from actual INTERMAGNET eclipse observations on a spherical Earth (Chapman mechanism), then labeled as dome predictions.

internal_issues.description: NEW: SEC-6.2: Unverifiable -29.1 nT claim in SEC-5.16 and again in the conclusions (line ~1446). 'monitor.py computes ΔB = −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −29.1 nT' — monitor.py is not archived in raw-text/, the FSF of 1.672 doesn't match Ebro's listed FSF (1.029), and the formula origin is unverifiable. Fix: Either (a) archive monitor.py and verify the specific line, or (b) replace with verifiable claim: W004-baseline predictions (HAD at −26.2 nT) diverge from the homepage's −17 to −21 nT framing.

suggested_patch.field: SEC-5.16 (line ~1446) and matching content in SEC-6.2
suggested_patch.find: But monitor.py computes ΔB = −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −29.1 nT. The published range and the computational model disagree by ~40%. Which prediction is being tested?
suggested_patch.replace: Yet the dome's own per-station predictions (e.g., HAD station: −26.2 nT) diverge from the '−17 to −21 nT' homepage framing by 25–55%. Without archiving monitor.py to verify the exact computation, the specific discrepancy cannot be confirmed — but the published prediction range itself already spans −5 to −26 nT across stations, making the headline '−17 to −21 nT' a misleading summary of a much wider interval.

internal_issues.description: NEW: SEC-6.2 conclusions never address the dome's four model-discriminating E-PRED tests (A through D). E-PRED-B (Hartland > Ebro despite lower spatial coverage) is dome-specific geometry and is the dome's strongest discriminating counterargument. Ignoring this in the conclusion exposes the review to: 'They never addressed our actual discriminating tests.' Fix: Add response to E-PRED tests in SEC-6.2, explaining why standard Chapman effects (local conductivity, Sq current geometry, latitude) explain station-to-station variation without dome-specific FSF.
